narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 36, which is right around the typical level for students in our dataset. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.6% chance that you might fail it—slightly above the threshold that led to your application being rejected this time. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile in Year 3 of school, where you ranked in the 1st decile, much lower than the average 5th or 6th decile for others, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar exam struggle. Your LSAT score helped counter that, pulling the prediction in a more favorable direction since it was competitive with peers. Other factors, like your middle family income bracket, also nudged slightly toward higher risk compared to typical applicants, while your Year 1 decile, undergraduate GPA of 2.60, and full-time student status offered minor positive influences but weren't enough to shift the balance. Overall, your profile stands out as having lower early academic rankings than most students who succeed on the bar, even with your LSAT holding steady. We know setbacks like this can feel especially heavy given the barriers many Black women face in higher education, but this isn't a reflection of your potential—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your grades to climb those school deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional coursework, and consider retaking the LSAT if you can edge it higher. Building a stronger undergraduate GPA equivalent through post-bac programs could also help. Reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and reapply once you've addressed these key areas—many students turn predictions like this around. You've got this; persistence is your superpower. Keep pushing forward.
